# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI — Brand & Codebase Design Guide — React Router Article Surfaces Version: 2.0 (current codebase adaptation) — Scope: branding, tokens, article component usage, import paths, routing, accessibility, and one-shot generation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form article content that compiles first try in the current MLAI codebase. Applies to: article content under `app/articles/content/**`, article support components under `app/components/articles/**`, marketing/page headers, and reusable resource/CTA blocks. --- ## 1) Current framework and architecture ### 1.1 Framework This repository is a **React Router v7 + React 19 + Vite + TypeScript** app. Use: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` Do **not** use: ```tsx import Link from 'next/link' import Image from 'next/image' ``` Do **not** use: - `use client` - `use server` - `export const metadata` - `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - Next.js App Router conventions - Vue/Svelte syntax ### 1.2 Article architecture Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` Article content files should export a default React component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Critical article rules: - **Do not import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`**. The route handler/layout layer already handles article layout concerns. - Do not add duplicate article title/header/breadcrumb/author sections if the route/layout renders them. - Body content should return a fragment (`<>...`) or a neutral wrapper. - If using a wrapper, use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`. - Use existing article design-system components instead of raw ad-hoc card/callout/image markup. - Do not use placeholder image services such as `placehold.co`. - Use real image URLs, `ARTICLE_FALLBACK_IMAGE` where appropriate inside supported components, or explicit placeholders like `` only in templates. - Ensure registry slug path and SEO config path match exactly. - Do not import `ArticleDisclaimer` in generated article content unless explicitly requested; article layout concerns are handled outside the body content. --- ## 2) Brand identity ### 2.1 Palette and observed codebase colours The codebase uses a mix of MLAI token variables and bold brutalist article colours. | Token / colour | Value / usage | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | observed in components | Bright teal surface/accent. Used in `ArticleStepList` bullets and brand borders. | | `--brand-ink` | observed in links | Teal link/hover colour on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | observed in components | Charcoal/dark text on bright brand surfaces. | | `--soft` | observed in components | Soft teal panel background. | | `--brutalist-orange` | CSS variable | Article/search filter orange. | | `--brutalist-purple` | CSS variable | Article/search filter purple. | | `--brutalist-black` | CSS variable | Article/search filter black. | | `--brutalist-yellow` | CSS variable | Article/search filter yellow. | | `--brutalist-beige` | CSS variable | Standalone page hero beige variant. | | `#ff3d00` | orange | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#4b1bd1` | purple | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#ffe900` | yellow | Brutalist yellow surface with black text. | | `#2b0f70` | deep purple | Resource CTA background. | | `#0d9488` | cyan/teal | Resource CTA background. | ### 2.2 Colour guardrails Use these existing patterns: - Purple surfaces: `bg-[#4b1bd1] text-white` - Orange surfaces: `bg-[#ff3d00] text-white` - Yellow surfaces: `bg-[#ffe900] text-black` - Transparent article panels: `bg-transparent border border-gray-300` or `border-gray-400` - Link emphasis on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline` - Neutral article text: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600` Do not: - Put `text-white` on yellow. - Use `bg-white` for generated article wrappers. - Invent new colour variants for existing components. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` on article design-system components. - Overuse bright colours in long reading sections. --- ## 3) Typography, spacing, and shape The codebase uses Tailwind utility classes and `@tailwindcss/typography`. Common article surface shapes: ```tsx rounded-[22px] rounded-[28px] rounded-[32px] rounded-2xl rounded-3xl ``` Common spacing: ```tsx my-8 my-12 mt-8 mt-10 mt-12 p-6 p-7 p-8 sm:p-7 md:p-9 ``` Common text classes: ```tsx text-gray-900 text-gray-800 text-gray-700 text-gray-600 text-sm text-base text-lg text-xl text-2xl text-3xl sm:text-4xl font-bold ``` --- ## 4) Styling system ### 4.1 Tailwind setup The current Tailwind config is minimal and uses the typography plugin: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography'; import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss'; export default { content: ["./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [ typography, ], } satisfies Config; ``` Use Tailwind classes directly in TSX. Do not introduce CSS modules or styled-components. ### 4.2 Import convention Prefer the configured alias: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Use relative imports only when matching an existing nearby file pattern is clearly necessary. --- ## 5) Routing and links Internal links use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles Read more ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx External source ``` Do not use `href` on React Router `Link`. Use `to`. --- ## 6) Article component rules Use the existing article design-system components below. Do not create raw card grids, raw quote boxes, or raw image blocks when a component exists. ### 6.1 Persona / audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. Import: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Do not hand-code persona cards with raw `
` border/shadow markup. --- ### 6.2 Quotes and callouts Use `QuoteBlock` for strong callouts and quotes. Supported props: ```ts type Props = { slug: string ctaText?: string className?: string descriptionOverride?: string variant?: 'default' | 'compact' authorOverride?: ArticleAuthor } ``` --- ### 6.11 Footer article nav Use `ArticleFooterNav` for end-of-article navigation if the article pattern requires it. Import: ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Avoid duplicate author blocks. --- ## 7) Page header components ### 7.1 Article body files Most article content files should not manually render a hero/header. # Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is a not-for-profit AI and machine learning community based in Australia. It brings together builders, learners, founders, researchers, students, clinicians, data scientists, AI developers, entrepreneurs, and industry supporters through events, hackathons, educational programs, community tools, and startup support initiatives.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial intelligence, machine learning, startup ecosystem, technology education/community  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation with events, educational programs, hackathons, and software-based founder/community tools  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia; broader relevance to “Australia and beyond” where AI community impact is discussed

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun, and impactful while fostering a vibrant Australian community passionate about AI and machine learning.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs and build a thriving AI ecosystem through meetups, hackathons, educational programs, practical community support, and meaningful professional connections.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, founders, students, clinicians, and practitioners.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world challenges, useful projects, and applied AI rather than abstract hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise members who help, volunteer, mentor, support founders, and share updates.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together people from different backgrounds — developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, healthcare professionals, students, sponsors, and mentors.
- **Transparency and momentum:** Encourage founders to build trust through consistent updates, clear asks, and visible progress.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use MLAI as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI and machine learning learners:** Students, early-career professionals, and curious beginners who want to understand AI, attend events, meet peers, and build confidence.
- **Builders and practitioners:** Data scientists, AI developers, software engineers, researchers, and technical contributors looking for practical challenges, hackathons, workshops, community support, and collaboration.
- **Startup founders and entrepreneurs:** Founders using AI or building technology companies who need pitch support, investor update habits, community feedback, introductions, and fundraising preparation.
- **Healthcare and medtech innovators:** Clinicians, doctors, nurses, pharmacists, healthcare professionals, students, data scientists, and AI builders interested in solving real healthcare bottlenecks through events such as MedHack.
- **Sponsors and ecosystem partners:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel intimidating, abstract, or hard to start with.
- Builders need real-world problems, collaborators, mentors, and structured opportunities to apply their skills.
- Founders often start raising money too late and do not build investor trust early enough.
- Early-stage founders struggle to communicate momentum, metrics, asks, risks, and updates clearly.
- Healthcare systems face bottlenecks around ageing populations, inpatient wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural care, and preventative medicine.
- Community members want simple ways to get involved, contribute meaningfully, and be recognised for helping.
- Sponsors need credible, values-aligned ways to support and reach the Australian AI ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, investors, founders, and technical peers.
- Build prototypes and solve real-world AI challenges through hackathons.
- Improve pitch clarity and communicate ideas effectively.
- Build investor relationships months before a raise.
- Turn community contribution into tangible perks such as coworking, events, mentoring, merch, and office hours.
- Support Australia’s AI and ML ecosystem through sponsorship, partnerships, and community involvement.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of MLAI’s offering as a community ecosystem made up of events, tools, hackathons, and contribution loops.

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   Meetups, workshops, social events, lightning talks, and educational gatherings across Australia for builders, learners, founders, and practitioners.

2. **Hackathons:**  
   Practical AI challenge events, including upcoming hackathons such as MedHack: Frontiers and the eSafety Hackathon. These events bring participants together to prototype solutions to real-world problems.

3. **MedHack: Frontiers:**  
   A healthcare AI hackathon focused on applying AI to pressing healthcare challenges. It brings clinicians, builders, data scientists, AI developers, mentors, judges, investors, and students together to prototype solutions across streams such as ageing populations, wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural healthcare, and preventative medicine.

4. **MLAI Vibe Raising:**  
   A founder tool that helps startups draft monthly investor updates from information the founder chooses to provide or optionally connect. It is designed to help founders build investor relationships before they raise by turning wins, asks, challenges, metrics, risks, and follow-ups into visible momentum.

5. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions and can be used for rewards such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, mentoring, merch, and other community perks.

6. **Founder education and resources:**  
   Guides and content such as “How to Pitch Your Big Idea” that help founders communicate complex ideas, use frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution, and close with clear calls to action.

7. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   Sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first, not just content-first:** MLAI is positioned around active participation, contribution, Slack involvement, volunteering, events, and practical support.
- **Practical AI focus:** Events and hackathons are tied to real-world challenges, not just theory or hype.
- **Cross-disciplinary collaboration:** MLAI brings together technical people, founders, students, researchers, clinicians, mentors, investors, and sponsors.
- **Founder support integrated into an AI community:** MLAI supports not only AI learning and building, but also startup pitching, investor updates, fundraising readiness, and founder visibility.
- **Contribution recognition through Roo Points:** MLAI has an internal system for recognising meaningful community contributions and turning them into practical perks.
- **Australian ecosystem focus:** The brand is explicitly focused on supporting Australia’s AI and ML community and making Australia a stronger home for AI entrepreneurship.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals — pick 1–2 per article, max:**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit community based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members” from diverse backgrounds including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals, and students.
- MLAI events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong community demand.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks, meetups, workshops, hackathons, and educational programs.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run in collaboration with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers uses Monash University Clayton Campus as a venue.
- MLAI positions itself as supporting Australia’s AI future and connecting sponsors with developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.
- MLAI Vibe Raising has privacy and terms pages covering Google OAuth, Gmail data use, connected sources, AI-assisted monthly founder updates, retention, deletion, and Limited Use commitments.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do not claim MLAI is a for-profit SaaS company; it is MLAI Aus Inc and is described as not-for-profit/non-profit.
- Do not claim Roo Points are money, wages, an exchange rate, or a guaranteed pathway to paid work. The site explicitly says they are internal community reward points with no cash value.
- Do not claim Vibe Raising provides financial, legal, tax, investment, medical, or professional advice.
- Do not claim AI-generated outputs from MLAI tools are always accurate; the terms state outputs may be inaccurate, incomplete, outdated, or misleading and must be reviewed.
- Do not claim MLAI sells personal information, Gmail data, Google user data, or connected-source data; the privacy policy says it does not.
- Do not imply Gmail or connected-data access is mandatory for Vibe Raising; connectors are optional.
- Do not invent sponsor names, award wins, funding numbers, partner lists, or member statistics beyond “over 10,000 members.”
- Do not claim participation in events, Roo Points, or volunteering guarantees jobs, team roles, event ownership, investor introductions, funding, or paid work.
- Do not overstate geographic reach beyond the stated Australian focus, though the site does mention “Australia and beyond” in relation to AI impact.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line

Choose one per article:

- “MLAI is a not-for-profit Australian community helping people learn, build, and connect around AI and machine learning.”
- “MLAI brings Australia’s AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, and partners together through events, hackathons, tools, and community programs.”
- “MLAI is an Australian AI and machine learning community focused on practical learning, real-world projects, and meaningful professional connections.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools, educational resources, and community contribution programs.”

### Primary CTAs

Use ONE per article, aligned to topic.

**For beginners/learners:**
> “Join an upcoming MLAI event to meet Australia’s AI and ML community and start learning with other builders.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Explore MLAI hackathons and build practical AI solutions with other developers, researchers, founders, and domain experts.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or relevant hackathon page such as `/medhack`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “Learn more about MedHack: Frontiers and how clinicians, builders, and AI practitioners are tackling real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “Use MLAI Vibe Raising to draft monthly investor updates and build investor relationships before you raise.”  
> Target: `/vibe-raising`

**For community members:**
> “Meet Roo, MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Connect with MLAI’s Australian AI community through events, hackathons, and educational programs.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`, depending on intent

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### URL Patterns or Pages to Link To

- Events: `https://mlai.au/events`
- Hackathons: `https://mlai.au/hackathons`
- MedHack: `https://mlai.au/medhack`
- Vibe Raising: `https://mlai.au/vibe-raising`
- Roo: `https://mlai.au/roo`
- Sponsors: `https://mlai.au/sponsors`
- Privacy policy for Vibe Raising / Google and Gmail data use: `https://mlai.au/privacy`
- Terms of service for Vibe Raising and connected-source use: `https://mlai.au/terms`
- Pitching guide: `https://mlai.au/how-to-pitch-your-idea`

### CTA Rules

- Use ONE CTA per article, either as a natural aside near the relevant section or as a final helpful next step.
- Match the CTA to the article’s topic and reader persona.
- Keep CTAs practical and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- For founder/fundraising articles, point to Vibe Raising or the pitching guide.
- For AI learning, community, and industry articles, point to events.
- For healthcare AI articles, point to MedHack.
- For sponsorship, ecosystem, or partnership articles, point to the sponsors page.
- For community participation and contribution articles, point to Roo.
- Mention privacy, optional connectors, and review requirements when discussing Vibe Raising in any data-sensitive context.

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use hard-sell language like “Buy now,” “Guarantee funding,” “Secure investors instantly,” or “Get paid through Roo Points.”
- Do not imply MLAI provides regulated professional advice.
- Do not push Vibe Raising as an automated replacement for founder judgement, investor relations, accounting, legal review, or financial advice.
- Do not direct every article to Vibe Raising; many MLAI readers are learners, builders, sponsors, or community members rather than founders.
- Do not promise event availability, acceptance, perks, mentoring, coworking, or rewards if capacity is limited.
- Do not imply joining Slack, attending an event, or earning Roo Points guarantees employment, sponsorship, investment, or leadership roles.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:**  
Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, accessible, optimistic, and slightly informal. MLAI’s tone is energetic and helpful without being overly corporate. It should feel like a capable community organiser speaking to founders, builders, learners, and partners.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over theoretical:** Focus on what people can build, learn, attend, draft, improve, or contribute.
- **Community-first:** Use language that invites participation and recognises contribution.
- **Accessible and encouraging:** Make AI feel approachable, especially for beginners and cross-disciplinary audiences.
- **Optimistic but grounded:** Celebrate AI’s potential while staying focused on real-world problems and responsible use.
- **Founder-aware:** For startup content, emphasise momentum, transparency, clear asks, investor trust, and starting early.
- **Technically accurate but not exclusionary:** Explain concepts clearly without dumbing them down or turning articles into jargon walls.

### Do’s

- Use plain-English explanations before technical depth.
- Use Australian context where relevant, especially for AI careers, events, community, startups, healthcare, and policy.
- Use community language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “members,” “mentors,” “contributors,” and “AI community.”
- Highlight practical formats: meetups, workshops, lightning talks, hackathons, Slack, founder updates, pitch nights, coworking, mentoring.
- When discussing pitching, use direct, useful frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution and clear calls to action.
- When discussing Vibe Raising, mention that founders can optionally connect Gmail or other sources and must review AI-generated outputs.
- When discussing Roo Points, describe them as internal community recognition or thank-you points, not money.
- Use clear CTAs that invite readers to attend, join, build, contribute, draft, learn, or partner.

### Don’ts

- Don’t sound like a generic enterprise AI vendor.
- Don’t overhype AI as a magic solution.
- Don’t present MLAI as a university, accelerator, recruitment agency, investment fund, or consulting firm unless future materials explicitly say so.
- Don’t imply MLAI owns or operates Monash University, MYMI, Google, Slack, Xero, Linear, Notion, or other third-party services.
- Don’t write in a patronising tone toward beginners.
- Don’t frame community contribution as unpaid labour-for-wages; Roo Points are recognition and perks, not compensation.
- Don’t make guarantees around fundraising, jobs, investment, AI accuracy, hackathon outcomes, or event acceptance.
- Don’t use excessive corporate buzzwords such as “synergistic transformation,” “AI revolution platform,” or “end-to-end disruption engine.”

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred for general articles, given the Australian focus. Preserve official names and quoted website wording where needed.
- **Preferred terms:**  
  AI, machine learning, AI community, Australian AI community, builders, learners, founders, contributors, mentors, hackathons, meetups, workshops, lightning talks, Roo, Roo Points, Vibe Raising, monthly investor updates, founder updates, meaningful contribution, community perks.
- **Avoided terms:**  
  Guaranteed funding, guaranteed job, cash value, wage, exchange rate, investment advice, legal advice, medical advice, professional advice, fully automated investor relations, instant investor access, replacement for review, AI certainty.

### Content Rules

- Position MLAI as a not-for-profit Australian AI and machine learning community, not as a generic SaaS vendor.
- Keep the core message practical: learn, build, connect, contribute, and solve real problems.
- Use the “over 10,000 members” proof point sparingly and accurately.
- For founder articles, emphasise building investor relationships before raising, monthly updates, transparency, metrics, asks, risks, and momentum.
- For healthcare AI articles, connect content to real healthcare bottlenecks and cross-disciplinary collaboration between clinicians and builders.
- For Roo-related articles, explain that Roo lives in Slack and helps manage contribution recognition, rewards, coworking, requests, and admin flows.
- For Vibe Raising articles, state that connected data sources such as Gmail are optional and that users choose what to provide or connect.
- Always include the review caveat for AI-assisted outputs when relevant.
- Avoid implying that MLAI’s tools, events, or community programs guarantee outcomes.
- Do not invent details about sponsorship tiers, sponsors, awards, leadership, founding year, funding, or event attendance numbers.
- Keep CTAs light, useful, and aligned to the reader’s likely next step.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI — Brand & Codebase Design Guide — React Router Article Surfaces Version: 2.0 (current codebase adaptation) — Scope: branding, tokens, article component usage, import paths, routing, accessibility, and one-shot generation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form article content that compiles first try in the current MLAI codebase. Applies to: article content under `app/articles/content/**`, article support components under `app/components/articles/**`, marketing/page headers, and reusable resource/CTA blocks. --- ## 1) Current framework and architecture ### 1.1 Framework This repository is a **React Router v7 + React 19 + Vite + TypeScript** app. Use: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` Do **not** use: ```tsx import Link from 'next/link' import Image from 'next/image' ``` Do **not** use: - `use client` - `use server` - `export const metadata` - `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - Next.js App Router conventions - Vue/Svelte syntax ### 1.2 Article architecture Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` Article content files should export a default React component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Critical article rules: - **Do not import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`**. The route handler/layout layer already handles article layout concerns. - Do not add duplicate article title/header/breadcrumb/author sections if the route/layout renders them. - Body content should return a fragment (`<>...`) or a neutral wrapper. - If using a wrapper, use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`. - Use existing article design-system components instead of raw ad-hoc card/callout/image markup. - Do not use placeholder image services such as `placehold.co`. - Use real image URLs, `ARTICLE_FALLBACK_IMAGE` where appropriate inside supported components, or explicit placeholders like `` only in templates. - Ensure registry slug path and SEO config path match exactly. - Do not import `ArticleDisclaimer` in generated article content unless explicitly requested; article layout concerns are handled outside the body content. --- ## 2) Brand identity ### 2.1 Palette and observed codebase colours The codebase uses a mix of MLAI token variables and bold brutalist article colours. | Token / colour | Value / usage | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | observed in components | Bright teal surface/accent. Used in `ArticleStepList` bullets and brand borders. | | `--brand-ink` | observed in links | Teal link/hover colour on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | observed in components | Charcoal/dark text on bright brand surfaces. | | `--soft` | observed in components | Soft teal panel background. | | `--brutalist-orange` | CSS variable | Article/search filter orange. | | `--brutalist-purple` | CSS variable | Article/search filter purple. | | `--brutalist-black` | CSS variable | Article/search filter black. | | `--brutalist-yellow` | CSS variable | Article/search filter yellow. | | `--brutalist-beige` | CSS variable | Standalone page hero beige variant. | | `#ff3d00` | orange | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#4b1bd1` | purple | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#ffe900` | yellow | Brutalist yellow surface with black text. | | `#2b0f70` | deep purple | Resource CTA background. | | `#0d9488` | cyan/teal | Resource CTA background. | ### 2.2 Colour guardrails Use these existing patterns: - Purple surfaces: `bg-[#4b1bd1] text-white` - Orange surfaces: `bg-[#ff3d00] text-white` - Yellow surfaces: `bg-[#ffe900] text-black` - Transparent article panels: `bg-transparent border border-gray-300` or `border-gray-400` - Link emphasis on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline` - Neutral article text: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600` Do not: - Put `text-white` on yellow. - Use `bg-white` for generated article wrappers. - Invent new colour variants for existing components. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` on article design-system components. - Overuse bright colours in long reading sections. --- ## 3) Typography, spacing, and shape The codebase uses Tailwind utility classes and `@tailwindcss/typography`. Common article surface shapes: ```tsx rounded-[22px] rounded-[28px] rounded-[32px] rounded-2xl rounded-3xl ``` Common spacing: ```tsx my-8 my-12 mt-8 mt-10 mt-12 p-6 p-7 p-8 sm:p-7 md:p-9 ``` Common text classes: ```tsx text-gray-900 text-gray-800 text-gray-700 text-gray-600 text-sm text-base text-lg text-xl text-2xl text-3xl sm:text-4xl font-bold ``` --- ## 4) Styling system ### 4.1 Tailwind setup The current Tailwind config is minimal and uses the typography plugin: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography'; import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss'; export default { content: ["./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [ typography, ], } satisfies Config; ``` Use Tailwind classes directly in TSX. Do not introduce CSS modules or styled-components. ### 4.2 Import convention Prefer the configured alias: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Use relative imports only when matching an existing nearby file pattern is clearly necessary. --- ## 5) Routing and links Internal links use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles Read more ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx External source ``` Do not use `href` on React Router `Link`. Use `to`. --- ## 6) Article component rules Use the existing article design-system components below. Do not create raw card grids, raw quote boxes, or raw image blocks when a component exists. ### 6.1 Persona / audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. Import: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Do not hand-code persona cards with raw `

    {TOPIC} can feel broad at first, but it becomes easier to understand when you connect it to practical problems, local communities, and small projects you can actually complete.

    This guide gives you a grounded way to approach the topic in Australia, with a focus on learning by doing and using credible sources.

    Start with a practical question

    The fastest way to make progress is to choose a specific question. For example, instead of asking how to learn everything about AI, ask what kind of AI system you want to understand, evaluate, build, or use.

    Treat {TOPIC} as something to practise, not just something to read about. A small project, a clear question, and a feedback loop will teach you more than a passive list of resources.

    Use credible Australian context

    Australian learners and founders should pay attention to local policy, privacy expectations, community events, and industry examples. Official and reputable sources can help you separate durable concepts from short-term hype.

    For broader context, start with Australian Government resources and reputable institutions such as{' '} CSIRO . You can also explore related MLAI articles in the{' '} article library .

    Build a feedback loop

    A useful feedback loop includes a project, a way to test your understanding, and a community or peer group that can challenge your assumptions. This helps you improve faster and avoid learning only in theory.

# Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is a not-for-profit AI and machine learning community based in Australia. It brings together builders, learners, founders, researchers, students, clinicians, data scientists, AI developers, entrepreneurs, and industry supporters through events, hackathons, educational programs, community tools, and startup support initiatives.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial intelligence, machine learning, startup ecosystem, technology education/community  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation with events, educational programs, hackathons, and software-based founder/community tools  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia; broader relevance to “Australia and beyond” where AI community impact is discussed

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun, and impactful while fostering a vibrant Australian community passionate about AI and machine learning.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs and build a thriving AI ecosystem through meetups, hackathons, educational programs, practical community support, and meaningful professional connections.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, founders, students, clinicians, and practitioners.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world challenges, useful projects, and applied AI rather than abstract hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise members who help, volunteer, mentor, support founders, and share updates.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together people from different backgrounds — developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, healthcare professionals, students, sponsors, and mentors.
- **Transparency and momentum:** Encourage founders to build trust through consistent updates, clear asks, and visible progress.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use MLAI as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI and machine learning learners:** Students, early-career professionals, and curious beginners who want to understand AI, attend events, meet peers, and build confidence.
- **Builders and practitioners:** Data scientists, AI developers, software engineers, researchers, and technical contributors looking for practical challenges, hackathons, workshops, community support, and collaboration.
- **Startup founders and entrepreneurs:** Founders using AI or building technology companies who need pitch support, investor update habits, community feedback, introductions, and fundraising preparation.
- **Healthcare and medtech innovators:** Clinicians, doctors, nurses, pharmacists, healthcare professionals, students, data scientists, and AI builders interested in solving real healthcare bottlenecks through events such as MedHack.
- **Sponsors and ecosystem partners:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel intimidating, abstract, or hard to start with.
- Builders need real-world problems, collaborators, mentors, and structured opportunities to apply their skills.
- Founders often start raising money too late and do not build investor trust early enough.
- Early-stage founders struggle to communicate momentum, metrics, asks, risks, and updates clearly.
- Healthcare systems face bottlenecks around ageing populations, inpatient wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural care, and preventative medicine.
- Community members want simple ways to get involved, contribute meaningfully, and be recognised for helping.
- Sponsors need credible, values-aligned ways to support and reach the Australian AI ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, investors, founders, and technical peers.
- Build prototypes and solve real-world AI challenges through hackathons.
- Improve pitch clarity and communicate ideas effectively.
- Build investor relationships months before a raise.
- Turn community contribution into tangible perks such as coworking, events, mentoring, merch, and office hours.
- Support Australia’s AI and ML ecosystem through sponsorship, partnerships, and community involvement.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of MLAI’s offering as a community ecosystem made up of events, tools, hackathons, and contribution loops.

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   Meetups, workshops, social events, lightning talks, and educational gatherings across Australia for builders, learners, founders, and practitioners.

2. **Hackathons:**  
   Practical AI challenge events, including upcoming hackathons such as MedHack: Frontiers and the eSafety Hackathon. These events bring participants together to prototype solutions to real-world problems.

3. **MedHack: Frontiers:**  
   A healthcare AI hackathon focused on applying AI to pressing healthcare challenges. It brings clinicians, builders, data scientists, AI developers, mentors, judges, investors, and students together to prototype solutions across streams such as ageing populations, wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural healthcare, and preventative medicine.

4. **MLAI Vibe Raising:**  
   A founder tool that helps startups draft monthly investor updates from information the founder chooses to provide or optionally connect. It is designed to help founders build investor relationships before they raise by turning wins, asks, challenges, metrics, risks, and follow-ups into visible momentum.

5. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions and can be used for rewards such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, mentoring, merch, and other community perks.

6. **Founder education and resources:**  
   Guides and content such as “How to Pitch Your Big Idea” that help founders communicate complex ideas, use frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution, and close with clear calls to action.

7. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   Sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first, not just content-first:** MLAI is positioned around active participation, contribution, Slack involvement, volunteering, events, and practical support.
- **Practical AI focus:** Events and hackathons are tied to real-world challenges, not just theory or hype.
- **Cross-disciplinary collaboration:** MLAI brings together technical people, founders, students, researchers, clinicians, mentors, investors, and sponsors.
- **Founder support integrated into an AI community:** MLAI supports not only AI learning and building, but also startup pitching, investor updates, fundraising readiness, and founder visibility.
- **Contribution recognition through Roo Points:** MLAI has an internal system for recognising meaningful community contributions and turning them into practical perks.
- **Australian ecosystem focus:** The brand is explicitly focused on supporting Australia’s AI and ML community and making Australia a stronger home for AI entrepreneurship.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals — pick 1–2 per article, max:**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit community based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members” from diverse backgrounds including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals, and students.
- MLAI events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong community demand.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks, meetups, workshops, hackathons, and educational programs.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run in collaboration with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers uses Monash University Clayton Campus as a venue.
- MLAI positions itself as supporting Australia’s AI future and connecting sponsors with developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.
- MLAI Vibe Raising has privacy and terms pages covering Google OAuth, Gmail data use, connected sources, AI-assisted monthly founder updates, retention, deletion, and Limited Use commitments.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do not claim MLAI is a for-profit SaaS company; it is MLAI Aus Inc and is described as not-for-profit/non-profit.
- Do not claim Roo Points are money, wages, an exchange rate, or a guaranteed pathway to paid work. The site explicitly says they are internal community reward points with no cash value.
- Do not claim Vibe Raising provides financial, legal, tax, investment, medical, or professional advice.
- Do not claim AI-generated outputs from MLAI tools are always accurate; the terms state outputs may be inaccurate, incomplete, outdated, or misleading and must be reviewed.
- Do not claim MLAI sells personal information, Gmail data, Google user data, or connected-source data; the privacy policy says it does not.
- Do not imply Gmail or connected-data access is mandatory for Vibe Raising; connectors are optional.
- Do not invent sponsor names, award wins, funding numbers, partner lists, or member statistics beyond “over 10,000 members.”
- Do not claim participation in events, Roo Points, or volunteering guarantees jobs, team roles, event ownership, investor introductions, funding, or paid work.
- Do not overstate geographic reach beyond the stated Australian focus, though the site does mention “Australia and beyond” in relation to AI impact.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line

Choose one per article:

- “MLAI is a not-for-profit Australian community helping people learn, build, and connect around AI and machine learning.”
- “MLAI brings Australia’s AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, and partners together through events, hackathons, tools, and community programs.”
- “MLAI is an Australian AI and machine learning community focused on practical learning, real-world projects, and meaningful professional connections.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools, educational resources, and community contribution programs.”

### Primary CTAs

Use ONE per article, aligned to topic.

**For beginners/learners:**
> “Join an upcoming MLAI event to meet Australia’s AI and ML community and start learning with other builders.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Explore MLAI hackathons and build practical AI solutions with other developers, researchers, founders, and domain experts.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or relevant hackathon page such as `/medhack`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “Learn more about MedHack: Frontiers and how clinicians, builders, and AI practitioners are tackling real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “Use MLAI Vibe Raising to draft monthly investor updates and build investor relationships before you raise.”  
> Target: `/vibe-raising`

**For community members:**
> “Meet Roo, MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Connect with MLAI’s Australian AI community through events, hackathons, and educational programs.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`, depending on intent

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### URL Patterns or Pages to Link To

- Events: `https://mlai.au/events`
- Hackathons: `https://mlai.au/hackathons`
- MedHack: `https://mlai.au/medhack`
- Vibe Raising: `https://mlai.au/vibe-raising`
- Roo: `https://mlai.au/roo`
- Sponsors: `https://mlai.au/sponsors`
- Privacy policy for Vibe Raising / Google and Gmail data use: `https://mlai.au/privacy`
- Terms of service for Vibe Raising and connected-source use: `https://mlai.au/terms`
- Pitching guide: `https://mlai.au/how-to-pitch-your-idea`

### CTA Rules

- Use ONE CTA per article, either as a natural aside near the relevant section or as a final helpful next step.
- Match the CTA to the article’s topic and reader persona.
- Keep CTAs practical and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- For founder/fundraising articles, point to Vibe Raising or the pitching guide.
- For AI learning, community, and industry articles, point to events.
- For healthcare AI articles, point to MedHack.
- For sponsorship, ecosystem, or partnership articles, point to the sponsors page.
- For community participation and contribution articles, point to Roo.
- Mention privacy, optional connectors, and review requirements when discussing Vibe Raising in any data-sensitive context.

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use hard-sell language like “Buy now,” “Guarantee funding,” “Secure investors instantly,” or “Get paid through Roo Points.”
- Do not imply MLAI provides regulated professional advice.
- Do not push Vibe Raising as an automated replacement for founder judgement, investor relations, accounting, legal review, or financial advice.
- Do not direct every article to Vibe Raising; many MLAI readers are learners, builders, sponsors, or community members rather than founders.
- Do not promise event availability, acceptance, perks, mentoring, coworking, or rewards if capacity is limited.
- Do not imply joining Slack, attending an event, or earning Roo Points guarantees employment, sponsorship, investment, or leadership roles.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:**  
Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, accessible, optimistic, and slightly informal. MLAI’s tone is energetic and helpful without being overly corporate. It should feel like a capable community organiser speaking to founders, builders, learners, and partners.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over theoretical:** Focus on what people can build, learn, attend, draft, improve, or contribute.
- **Community-first:** Use language that invites participation and recognises contribution.
- **Accessible and encouraging:** Make AI feel approachable, especially for beginners and cross-disciplinary audiences.
- **Optimistic but grounded:** Celebrate AI’s potential while staying focused on real-world problems and responsible use.
- **Founder-aware:** For startup content, emphasise momentum, transparency, clear asks, investor trust, and starting early.
- **Technically accurate but not exclusionary:** Explain concepts clearly without dumbing them down or turning articles into jargon walls.

### Do’s

- Use plain-English explanations before technical depth.
- Use Australian context where relevant, especially for AI careers, events, community, startups, healthcare, and policy.
- Use community language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “members,” “mentors,” “contributors,” and “AI community.”
- Highlight practical formats: meetups, workshops, lightning talks, hackathons, Slack, founder updates, pitch nights, coworking, mentoring.
- When discussing pitching, use direct, useful frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution and clear calls to action.
- When discussing Vibe Raising, mention that founders can optionally connect Gmail or other sources and must review AI-generated outputs.
- When discussing Roo Points, describe them as internal community recognition or thank-you points, not money.
- Use clear CTAs that invite readers to attend, join, build, contribute, draft, learn, or partner.

### Don’ts

- Don’t sound like a generic enterprise AI vendor.
- Don’t overhype AI as a magic solution.
- Don’t present MLAI as a university, accelerator, recruitment agency, investment fund, or consulting firm unless future materials explicitly say so.
- Don’t imply MLAI owns or operates Monash University, MYMI, Google, Slack, Xero, Linear, Notion, or other third-party services.
- Don’t write in a patronising tone toward beginners.
- Don’t frame community contribution as unpaid labour-for-wages; Roo Points are recognition and perks, not compensation.
- Don’t make guarantees around fundraising, jobs, investment, AI accuracy, hackathon outcomes, or event acceptance.
- Don’t use excessive corporate buzzwords such as “synergistic transformation,” “AI revolution platform,” or “end-to-end disruption engine.”

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred for general articles, given the Australian focus. Preserve official names and quoted website wording where needed.
- **Preferred terms:**  
  AI, machine learning, AI community, Australian AI community, builders, learners, founders, contributors, mentors, hackathons, meetups, workshops, lightning talks, Roo, Roo Points, Vibe Raising, monthly investor updates, founder updates, meaningful contribution, community perks.
- **Avoided terms:**  
  Guaranteed funding, guaranteed job, cash value, wage, exchange rate, investment advice, legal advice, medical advice, professional advice, fully automated investor relations, instant investor access, replacement for review, AI certainty.

### Content Rules

- Position MLAI as a not-for-profit Australian AI and machine learning community, not as a generic SaaS vendor.
- Keep the core message practical: learn, build, connect, contribute, and solve real problems.
- Use the “over 10,000 members” proof point sparingly and accurately.
- For founder articles, emphasise building investor relationships before raising, monthly updates, transparency, metrics, asks, risks, and momentum.
- For healthcare AI articles, connect content to real healthcare bottlenecks and cross-disciplinary collaboration between clinicians and builders.
- For Roo-related articles, explain that Roo lives in Slack and helps manage contribution recognition, rewards, coworking, requests, and admin flows.
- For Vibe Raising articles, state that connected data sources such as Gmail are optional and that users choose what to provide or connect.
- Always include the review caveat for AI-assisted outputs when relevant.
- Avoid implying that MLAI’s tools, events, or community programs guarantee outcomes.
- Do not invent details about sponsorship tiers, sponsors, awards, leadership, founding year, funding, or event attendance numbers.
- Keep CTAs light, useful, and aligned to the reader’s likely next step.

# ArticleLayout — React Router Specification

## Purpose
Main React Router article wrapper with optional sidebar for TOC, breadcrumbs, structured data, related content, featured professionals, FAQ content, and next article navigation.

In React Router, route-level SEO metadata should be exposed from the route module using `meta`. JSON-LD structured data may be rendered by `ArticleLayout` using `<script type="application/ld+json">`.

## File
Use `.tsx`.

Recommended location:

```txt
app/components/article-layout.tsx
```

Route usage example:

```txt
app/routes/articles.$slug.tsx
```

## Props

### Required
| Prop | Type | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| `article` | `ArticleMetadata` | Article metadata object with title, description, slug, date, author, etc. |
| `children` | `ReactNode` | Article content to render inside the layout |

### Optional
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| `showHero` | `boolean` | `true` | Whether to show hero section |
| `showDate` | `boolean` | `true` | Whether to display publication date |
| `showDisclaimer` | `boolean` | `false` | Whether to show legal disclaimer |
| `breadcrumb` | `string` | - | Single breadcrumb text |
| `breadcrumbItems` | `BreadcrumbItem[]` | - | Array of breadcrumb items |
| `titleTag` | `'h1' \| 'h2'` | `'h1'` | HTML tag for title |
| `featuredProfessionals` | `Professional[]` | - | Array of featured professionals to display |
| `featuredProfessionalsTitle` | `string` | - | Title for professionals section |
| `featuredProfessionalsPersona` | `string` | - | Persona filter for professionals |
| `faqItems` | `FAQItem[]` | - | FAQ items to display and add to schema |
| `howTo` | `HowToSchema` | - | HowTo structured data |
| `summaryHighlights` | `string[]` | - | Key points summary list |
| `containerMaxWidthClassName` | `string` | - | Override max-width for outer container |
| `contentMaxWidthClassName` | `string` | - | Override max-width for content area |
| `containerClassName` | `string` | - | Additional classes for container |
| `containerInnerClassName` | `string` | - | Additional classes for inner container |
| `contentPaddingClassName` | `string` | - | Override padding classes |
| `upcomingEvents` | `Event[]` | - | Events to display in sidebar |
| `showHeader` | `boolean` | `true` | Whether to show page header |

## Semantic Hints
`layout`, `article`, `wrapper`, `container`, `page-layout`, `article-page`, `article-container`, `prose`, `content-wrapper`

## When to Use
Use as the outermost rendered wrapper inside every article route component. Fetch article data in the React Router `loader`, define route metadata in `meta`, then pass loaded article metadata into `ArticleLayout`.

## React Router Structure

```txt
route module
├── loader: fetch article metadata/content data
├── meta: return title/description/canonical metadata
└── default route component
    └── ArticleLayout
        ├── structured data script
        ├── Header optio

# ArticleEnhancer

## Purpose
React Router client-only article enhancer that scans rendered article content for headings, generates a table of contents, and injects HowTo/MediaObject JSON-LD structured data. Uses `MutationObserver` to handle dynamically rendered content.

## File
Use a client-specific file so the component is never imported into server-only code:

```txt
app/components/article-enhancer.client.tsx
```

Do not add a Next.js-style `'use client'` directive.

## Props

### Required
| Prop | Type | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| `articleTitle` | `string` | Title of the article for structured data |
| `contentSelector` | `string` | CSS selector for the article content container |

### Optional
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| `articleDescription` | `string` | - | Description for structured data |
| `enableToc` | `boolean` | `true` | Whether to generate table of contents |
| `enableHowTo` | `boolean` | `true` | Whether to inject HowTo schema |
| `enableMediaObject` | `boolean` | `true` | Whether to inject MediaObject schema for images |
| `children` | `ReactNode` | - | Article content to wrap |

## Semantic Hints
`enhancer`, `wrapper`, `article-wrapper`, `enhancement`, `seo`, `structured-data`, `meta`

## When to Use
Wrap article content when a React Router route needs automatic TOC generation and client-generated structured data. This is usually handled internally by an article layout route/component.

## Behavior
1. **On mount**: scan `contentSelector` for `h2` and `h3` headings.
2. **TOC generation**:
   - Extract heading text and IDs.
   - Remove emoji from heading text.
   - Assign stable slug IDs to headings without IDs.
   - Group `h3` items under the previous `h2`.
   - Render the TOC into an `#article-toc-placeholder` element with `createPortal`.
3. **HowTo schema**:
   - Detect ordered step lists in the article content.
   - Generate HowTo JSON-LD using `articleTitle` and optional `articleDescription`.
4. **MediaObject schema**:
   - Find images inside article content.
   - Generate MediaObject JSON-LD for each image.
5. **MutationObserver**:
   - Re-scan after content changes.
   - Debounce observer callbacks.
6. **Cleanup**:
   - Disconnect the observer.
   - Clear pending timers.
   - Remove JSON-LD scripts injected by this component.

## Structure
```tsx
<ArticleEnhancer>
    ├── JSON-LD scripts appended to document.head
    │   ├── HowTo JSON-LD
    │   └── MediaObject JSON-LD
    ├── TOC portal rendered into #article-toc-placeholder
    └── {children}
</ArticleEnhancer>
```

## Implementation Notes
- Implement with `useEffect`; never access `document`, `window`, or `MutationObserver` during render.
- Use `createPortal` from `react-dom`.
- Store generated JSON-LD scripts with a data attribute, e.g. `data-article-enhancer-schema`, so cleanup is deterministic.
- Prefer precise TypeScript props:

```tsx
import type { ReactNode } from 'react';

export type Article

spec.title: What Is a Startup Company?
spec.meta_description: What is a startup company? Learn the basics for future AI founders, including scale, validation, funding and the difference from a small business.

summary_items.label: What is considered a startup company?
summary_items.description: A startup company is an early-stage company or project created to develop and validate a scalable business model. It is considered a startup when founders are still testing the product, market and growth model.

summary_items.label: What exactly is a startup company?
summary_items.description: A startup company is a new venture trying to prove that its product, service, customers and revenue model can grow. The core feature is search, not simply being newly formed.

summary_items.label: What is the difference between a startup and a company?
summary_items.description: A startup is usually a company in the early search phase for a scalable business model. A company can also be a stable local business, self-employment venture or established operation without rapid growth intent.

summary_question_candidates.question: What is considered a startup company?
What exactly is a startup company?
What is the difference between a startup and a company?

faq_items.question: Can an AI demo be a startup?
faq_items.answer: An AI demo can become startup-like only when it connects to a real customer problem, a defined customer group and a growth model. A useful technical capability alone is not enough.

faq_items.question: Who usually starts a startup?
faq_items.answer: A startup is usually initiated by a founder or small founding team that builds the first version and tests whether a market wants it. Early teams often work with limited resources.

faq_items.question: Why do startups fail?
faq_items.answer: Startups often fail because they face high uncertainty around the customer, product, market or growth path. Weak market need, unclear customers and a business model that cannot scale are major risks.

faq_items.question: Should a startup raise funding early?
faq_items.answer: Funding can help a startup hire, build and grow faster, but it is not proof that the business works. The better order is to validate the model first, then use funding as fuel.

sections.heading: A startup is a business model in search mode
paragraphs: A startup company is an early-stage company or project created to develop and validate a scalable business model. It is not just a new business. The key idea is search: the founders are testing whether a product or service can solve a real problem, reach a market, and grow beyond the first team.

That makes a startup different from many small businesses. A small business may be designed to serve a stable local market. A startup usually aims to grow quickly, often with limited resources, by bringing a new product or service to market. For future AI founders, the practical test is simple: can the technology become a repeatable product or service that real customers want?
bullets: Early-stage: it is still proving the idea, market, and model.
Scalable: it is designed to grow beyond one founder or one local operation.
Market-facing: the technology must connect to a real customer need.

sections.heading: The simplest definition of a startup company
paragraphs: A startup company is a new company or project created to seek, develop, and validate a scalable business model. In plain English, it is a new venture trying to prove that its product, service, customers, and revenue model can grow beyond a small local operation or a solo founder.

This is why a startup is not the same as every new business. Entrepreneurship includes many kinds of new businesses, including self-employment and small businesses that may not aim for rapid growth. A startup is usually early-stage, resource-constrained, and uncertain. Its founders are still testing whether the idea can work at scale.

callout.title: Simple test
callout.body: Not every new business is a startup.

sections.heading: How startups differ from ordinary new businesses
paragraphs: A startup is not just any new company. Ordinary new businesses can include self-employment, a local service business, or a company built to serve a steady market. A startup is usually built around the search for a scalable business model.

That growth intent changes the work. A small business may aim to become stable and profitable in a known market. A startup usually aims to bring a new product or service to market, create a new niche, or challenge how an existing market works. This is why startups are often linked with innovation, problem-solving, and disruption. But innovation alone is not enough. The idea still needs a business model that can repeatedly create value for customers.

The other major difference is uncertainty. Early startups often operate with limited resources and incomplete evidence. They may not yet know which customer segment will pay, which product shape will work, or which path to growth is realistic. A project becomes more startup-like when the founder starts testing those questions with customers and uses the results to validate or change the model.

callout.title: Early-stage reality
callout.body: Scale is not the same as instant growth. The early startup job is to reduce uncertainty through customer evidence and a clearer business model.

subsections.heading: Growth intent
paragraphs: Startups are usually designed to scale, while many ordinary new businesses are designed to operate well at a smaller size. Scale does not mean instant growth. It means the founder is looking for a model that could grow beyond the first few sales.

subsections.heading: Uncertainty and innovation
paragraphs: Startups often begin with a new idea, a new technology, or a different way to solve a customer problem.

sections.heading: What future AI founders should validate first
paragraphs: For an AI founder, the first question is not only whether the technology works. A startup is a company or project seeking to develop and validate a scalable business model. That means the idea needs more than a working model, demo, or prototype. It needs a real problem, a customer group, and a path to growth.

Startups are usually early-stage businesses built around innovation, problem-solving, and uncertainty. Future AI founders should treat that uncertainty as something to test. Before calling an AI idea a startup, ask whether the product or service solves a problem that a repeatable group of customers actually has. Then ask whether the same solution could grow beyond one custom project or one founder-led service.

A strong AI capability can still fail as a startup if the market need is weak, the customer is unclear, or the business model cannot scale. The practical goal is to connect the AI use case to a customer need and a business model that can grow.

callout.title: Founder check
callout.body: A useful AI demo is not automatically a startup; it needs a customer, use case, and growth logic.

subsections.heading: Validate the problem before the product
paragraphs: A future AI founder should begin by testing the problem, the customer, and the market. The product matters, but a startup is judged by whether it can bring a new product or service to market and scale. If the idea only proves that an AI system can do something interesting, it is still missing the business test.

sections.heading: Funding, ownership and risk in the early stage
paragraphs: Startups often begin with limited resources. A founder or small founding team may have the idea, build the first version, and test whether a market wants it. External funding, including venture capital, can help a startup move faster, hire people, or build more product. But funding is not the same as proof that the business works.

Funding choices can also shape the company. Investors may expect fast growth, clearer milestones, and a path to a scalable business model. That can be useful when the model is validated, but it can add pressure if the startup is still searching for the right customer, product, or market. In the early stage, the better order is to validate the idea first, then use funding as fuel.

Risk is part of the startup definition. Early-stage startups face significant uncertainty and many do not succeed. That is why founders should avoid overcommitting too early. A startup is still a search for a model that can grow, so each major spend, hire, or funding decision should be tied to what has been learned from real users or customers.

callout.title: Funding is fuel, not proof
callout.body: Treat funding as fuel for a validated model, not as proof that the startup is already working.

sections.heading: A practical path from idea to startup
paragraphs: A practical startup path starts with the customer problem, not the company structure. A startup is usually an early-stage company or project that is trying to develop and validate a scalable business model. That means the first job is to define who has the problem, why it matters, and whether the proposed product or service could serve more than one customer or one custom project.

The next phase is demand validation. Before building too much, hiring too quickly, or seeking funding, founders need evidence that the idea solves a real need. The order matters because startups operate with limited resources and face high uncertainty.

Once there is early evidence, the founder can shape the model and choose the next milestone. The model should explain how the startup could grow beyond the founder’s direct effort, because scalability is part of what separates a startup from many ordinary new businesses. The next milestone might be a clearer launch plan, a sharper product direction, or a stronger case for support. The goal is not to complete every business task at once. It is to move from idea to evidence, then from evidence to a more focused startup direction.

callout.title: Keep the process lean
callout.body: Use validation and lifecycle thinking as a guide.

sections.heading: Start with evidence, not the label
paragraphs: A startup is not just a young company with a modern brand. It is an early-stage company or project trying to seek, develop, and validate a scalable business model. That means uncertainty is part of the job. The label matters less than whether a real customer problem, a possible market, and a path to growth can be tested.

For a future AI founder, the next move is practical: validate the problem before treating the technology as the company. Funding can help a startup grow, but it does not remove the need for market evidence. Start with one customer group, one clear problem, and one assumption about how the model could scale. Then test whether the market gives you a reason to keep building.
bullets: Define the customer problem before pitching the product.
Check whether the business model can grow beyond one-off work.
Use funding as support for growth, not as proof that the startup is working.

cta.title: Build with clearer startup assumptions
cta.body: Start by testing one customer problem, one repeatable customer group and one scalable model assumption before you overbuild, hire or fundraise.
